metadata.summary: Use pnpm 9+ workspaces with catalog: protocol to share dependency versions across packages. One entry in pnpm-workspace.yaml controls versions for all packages; eliminates AI-agent version drift.

content.title: pnpm Workspaces with Dependency Catalogs
content.summary: In a TypeScript / JavaScript monorepo, use pnpm 9+ with `workspaces` and the `catalog:` protocol to share external dependency versions across packages. One catalog entry in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` controls the version of `react`, `typescript`, `vitest`, etc., for every workspace package; every `package.json` references those entries with `"react": "catalog:"`. Internal packages link via `"workspace:*"`. The result: zero version drift, one bump updates the world, AI agents cannot accidentally introduce a `react@19.0` / `react@19.1` split by adding a dep to one package.

why.p: Multi-package monorepos accumulate "package A wants react 19.0, package B wants react 19.1" merge conflicts because every `package.json` is a private little manifest that diverges over time. Catalogs invert that: the version lives in one place, the manifests reference it. AI agents adding deps via `pnpm add react -F web` automatically pick up the catalog version (when configured), so the next agent on a different package sees the same React. pnpm's filtering (`--filter`) and strict peer-dep model also makes hermetic per-package builds tractable, which is the prerequisite for parallel AI worktrees.

list.item: Any monorepo with two or more packages.
Any team that has fought "package X wants react@19.0, package Y wants react@19.1" merge conflicts.
Microfrontend / shared-library workspaces where consistency is an audit concern.
Repos with parallel AI agents in worktrees that may add deps independently.
Single-package apps where npm or Bun is enough — catalogs add ceremony without payoff.
Bun-native runtime projects that need `bun.lockb` parity with the runtime — use `bun install`.
Yarn 4 PnP shops with working constraints — the migration cost rarely beats the status quo.
Hybrid setups that must publish to npm with classic `dependencies` (publish step must replace `catalog:` and `workspace:*` with concrete versions; add a release script before adopting).

section.name: catalog-protocol
section.p: The pnpm catalog is a central version registry for the workspace. A `package.json` referencing `"react": "catalog:"` resolves to whatever version is declared at the workspace root. Default catalog entries use the bare `catalog:` literal; named catalogs use `catalog:<name>` for variants (e.g., a "next" catalog for canary versions).

rule.statement: External dependencies that appear in two or more workspace packages MUST be declared once under `catalog:` (or a named catalog) in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` and referenced with `"<pkg>": "catalog:"` in every package.json. Internal workspace packages MUST be referenced as `"<pkg>": "workspace:*"`. Hard-coded versions (e.g., `"react": "^19.0.0"`) for shared deps are forbidden.
rule.rationale: Catalogs make version drift impossible by construction: there is exactly one place a version is defined. Without catalogs, agents adding the same dep in two packages will pick whatever range is current that day, producing duplicate trees and subtle compatibility breakages.

example.code: # pnpm-workspace.yaml packages: - 'packages/*' - 'apps/*' catalog: react: ^19.0.0 react-dom: ^19.0.0 typescript: ^5.7.0 vitest: ^3.0.0 catalogs: next: react: 19.1.0-canary
example.note: Bumping React = single line change in pnpm-workspace.yaml; every workspace updates atomically on `pnpm install`.
Two React versions, two trees, runtime breakage when shared components cross the boundary.

antipattern.description: Adding a new shared dep via `pnpm add react -F web@^19.1.2` without updating the catalog.
antipattern.reason: Bypasses the catalog and reintroduces drift. Add deps via `pnpm add react -F web` (it picks up the catalog default) or update the catalog first if a new version is needed.

section.name: filter-patterns
section.p: `pnpm --filter` (or `-F`) lets the agent operate on one package, on a path glob, on packages affected by a git ref, or on the dependency closure of a target. Critical for keeping inner-loop time bounded in a large monorepo.

rule.statement: Use `--filter ...{path}` (with the leading dots) to operate on a package AND all of its dependents; `--filter {path}^...` for a package and its dependencies. CI uses `--filter ...[origin/main]` to test only what changed since main.
rule.rationale: Running `pnpm test` workspace-wide on every commit is unnecessary in monorepos with dozens of packages; filtered runs cut wall-clock time 5–20x while still exercising the affected slice. The leading-dots syntax is exact and standard; agents must use it instead of greps and shell glue.

example.code:
# install only what `web` needs pnpm install --filter web^...
# test web and everything that depends on it pnpm --filter ...web test
# test only packages changed since origin/main pnpm --filter '...[origin/main]' test
# add a runtime dep to a single package, picking up the catalog version pnpm add react -F web
example.note: Filters are precise; the agent never has to glob package paths by hand.

antipattern.description: Running `pnpm -r test` on every CI run when only one package changed.
antipattern.reason: Wastes CI minutes and floods the agent's feedback loop with unrelated output. Use `--filter '...[origin/main]'` so the slice is automatic and minimal.

template.purpose: Workspace + catalog declaration with the most-shared packages.
